architecture.summary: HRPO is an RL-based framework that trains LLMs to perform hybrid latent reasoning by blending discrete token embeddings with continuous hidden state representations. During rollouts, a learned gating mechanism (Equation 4) progressively incorporates prior layer hidden states into sampled token embeddings, initialized to favor tokens and gradually incorporating latent features. The gated hybrid input feeds into the next decoding step. Policy optimization uses REINFORCE-style gradient updates (Equation 6) with outcome-based rewards and KL regularization, computing log probabilities over token IDs. The approach maintains end-to-end differentiability via weighted interpolation of hidden states into the embedding manifold (Equation 3) and preserves standard autoregressive decoding for final answer generation.
trained_components: gating mechanism (gate vectors and scaling constant)

biblio.title: Hybrid Latent Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning

claims.dataset: knowledge benchmarks (averaged NQ, TriviaQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMQA, Bamboogle)
claims.statement: HRPO reaches 0.380 EM with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest 7B RAG baseline by 4.5%
HRPO with 1.5B backbone achieves average of 0.337, outperforming PPO by 3.0%

claims.dataset: STEM benchmarks (GSM8k, MATH, MATH500, MMLU-ST, ARC-C)
claims.statement: HRPO with 3B backbone achieves average accuracy of 0.700 on STEM benchmarks, matching the best 7B baseline on four of the datasets
HRPO with 1.5B backbone on MATH achieves 0.518, improving over GRPO baseline by 1.6%

HRPO-trained LLMs exhibit intriguing behaviors including cross-lingual patterns that fluidly integrate tokens from different languages, suggesting latent representations generalize across linguistic boundaries

The gating mechanism initializes to prioritize sampled token embeddings and progressively incorporates hidden features during training, enabling smooth integration of continuous representations while preserving LLM generative capabilities
HRPO introduces additional computation overhead, the on-policy design may reduce large-scale training efficiency, and its continuous representations can be less transparent

Direct injection of hidden states without interpolation degrades generation and yields nonsensical rollouts with zero reward due to mismatch between hidden state and embedding manifolds; interpolation without gating eventually suffers reward collapse
